title: Entity Categories
concept: Typical categories: Person (PER), Organization (ORG), Location (LOC), and Date/Time (DATE).

exercises.prompt: Why do we categorize entities in text?

options.text: To count the total number of letters in a book
To group words into types like people, places, and organizations
To translate text into a different language

exercises.explain: We categorize entities into groups like Person, Organization, and Location so the computer knows exactly *what* kind of thing a word refers to.

cards.title: Sorting the World
cards.body: When you read 'Paris', you instantly know it's a place. When you see 'Google', you know it's a company. But how do computer systems group these words automatically? They use Entity Categories to organize the messy world of human language!

cards.title: What are Entity Categories?
cards.body: Entity Categories are the standard labels we use to group real-world objects in text. The most common ones are Person (PER) for people, Organization (ORG) for groups/companies, Location (LOC) for places, and Date/Time (DATE) for calendar points.

cards.title: The Mail Sorter
cards.body: Imagine sorting your mail. You put letters from friends into the 'People' pile, bills from companies into the 'Business' pile, and postcards into the 'Places' pile. Named Entity Recognition does the exact same thing with words, sorting them into labeled piles like PER, ORG, and LOC!

cards.title: A Sentenced Tagged
cards.body:
Look at this sentence: 'Alice visited NASA in Florida last Tuesday.'
• Alice ➔ Person (PER)
• NASA ➔ Organization (ORG)
• Florida ➔ Location (LOC)
• last Tuesday ➔ Date/Time (DATE)

cards.title: One Word, One Category?
cards.body: You might think a word has only one category forever. But context is key! 'Apple' is an ORG (Organization) when talking about iPhones, but it's just a regular fruit (not a named entity at all) when you eat it for lunch!

cards.title: Spot the Differences
cards.body: Different entities need different tags. Mixing them up confuses the AI! Look at how we separate people and organizations from places and dates.

exercises.prompt: Which of these correctly pairs the words with their entity categories?
exercises.explain: PER stands for Person, which matches 'Albert Einstein'. ORG stands for Organization, which matches 'Princeton University'.

exercises.prompt: True or False: The word 'Monday' should be categorized as LOC (Location).
exercises.explain: Dates, months, years, and specific times are categorized under the DATE label, not LOC (which is for geographic places).

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing entity category tag for Paris:
exercises.sentence: Marie Curie [PER] was born in Paris [blank].
exercises.explain: Since Paris is a physical city/place, it falls under the Location (LOC) category, while Marie Curie is a Person (PER).

exercises.prompt: An AI is categorizing names in a document. Which classification is correct?
exercises.explain: Option B is incorrect because 'London' is a Location (LOC), not an Organization (ORG). Option C is incorrect because 'Microsoft' is a company (ORG), not a Person (PER). Option A correctly labels the person as PER.

exercises.prompt: A smart assistant scans the text: 'On June 15, 2012, UNICEF visited Mount Everest.' What entity categories should it assign to 'June 15, 2012', 'UNICEF', and 'Mount Everest' in that exact order?
exercises.explain: 'June 15, 2012' is a specific point in time, which fits the DATE category. 'UNICEF' is a group/institution, which is an Organization (ORG). 'Mount Everest' is a physical geographic point, which is a Location (LOC).
